speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Now, my co-mates and brothers in exile,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Hath not old custom made this life more sweet

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Than that of painted pomp? Are not these woods

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: More free from peril than the envious court?

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Here feel we not the penalty of Adam,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: The seasons’ difference, as the icy fang

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: And churlish chiding of the winter’s wind,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Which when it bites and blows upon my body

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Even till I shrink with cold, I smile and say

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: “This is no flattery. These are counselors

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: That feelingly persuade me what I am.”

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Sweet are the uses of adversity,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Which, like the toad, ugly and venomous,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Wears yet a precious jewel in his head.

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: And this our life, exempt from public haunt,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Finds tongues in trees, books in the running brooks,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Sermons in stones, and good in everything.

speaker: AMIENS
text: I would not change it. Happy is your Grace,

speaker: AMIENS
text: That can translate the stubbornness of fortune

speaker: AMIENS
text: Into so quiet and so sweet a style.

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Come, shall we go and kill us venison?

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: And yet it irks me the poor dappled fools,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Being native burghers of this desert city,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Should in their own confines with forkèd heads

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Have their round haunches gored.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Indeed, my lord,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: The melancholy Jaques grieves at that,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: And in that kind swears you do more usurp

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Than doth your brother that hath banished you.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Today my Lord of Amiens and myself

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Did steal behind him as he lay along

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Under an oak, whose antique root peeps out

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Upon the brook that brawls along this wood;

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: To the which place a poor sequestered stag

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: That from the hunter’s aim had ta’en a hurt

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Did come to languish. And indeed, my lord,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: The wretched animal heaved forth such groans

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: That their discharge did stretch his leathern coat

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Almost to bursting, and the big round tears

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Coursed one another down his innocent nose

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: In piteous chase. And thus the hairy fool,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Much markèd of the melancholy Jaques,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Stood on th’ extremest verge of the swift brook,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Augmenting it with tears.

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: But what said Jaques?

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Did he not moralize this spectacle?

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: O yes, into a thousand similes.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: First, for his weeping into the needless stream:

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: “Poor deer,” quoth he, “thou mak’st a testament

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: As worldlings do, giving thy sum of more

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: To that which had too much.” Then, being there alone,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Left and abandoned of his velvet friends:

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: “’Tis right,” quoth he. “Thus misery doth part

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: The flux of company.” Anon a careless herd,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Full of the pasture, jumps along by him

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: And never stays to greet him. “Ay,” quoth Jaques,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: “Sweep on, you fat and greasy citizens.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: ’Tis just the fashion. Wherefore do you look

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Upon that poor and broken bankrupt there?”

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Thus most invectively he pierceth through

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: The body of country, city, court,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Yea, and of this our life, swearing that we

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: Are mere usurpers, tyrants, and what’s worse,

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: To fright the animals and to kill them up

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: In their assigned and native dwelling place.

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: And did you leave him in this contemplation?

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: We did, my lord, weeping and commenting

speaker:
SECOND
               
              LORD
text: Upon the sobbing deer.

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: Show me the place.

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: I love to cope him in these sullen fits,

speaker:
DUKE
               
              SENIOR
text: For then he’s full of matter.

speaker:
FIRST
               
              LORD
text: I’ll bring you to him straight.
